coredata.dc:title: S and N codoped graphene quantum dots decorated on (001)TiO2 to boost surface reaction for photocatalytic hydrogen production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Mengyao
Yu, Shan
Zhong, Yunqian
Chen, Yijiang
Duan, Chao
Guo, Heng
Zhou, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) composites are popular photocatalysts for hydrogen production from water. Here we report a composite consisting of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) with (001)TiO2 for photocatalytic hydrogen production. In addition to traditional advantages like light absorption promotion and charge transfer improvement, the as-prepared SN-GQDs/(001)TiO2 composite could significantly enhance the surface reaction by promotion of the formation of hydroxyl radicals, which results in the increase of the hydrogen production activity by 40 times. This work reveals the importance of surface reaction promotion by GQDs for hydrogen production, which will help researchers understand relevant systems comprehensively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
TiO2
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen production
Surface reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization results of 001T and SN-GQD/001T: (a) XRD, (b) Raman, (c) TEM and (d) High-resolution TEM.
XPS spectra and H2O-TPD results of SN-GQD/001T composites. (a) Full survey of the sample. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s; (c) O1s (d) N1s and (e) S2p; H2O-TPD results of (f) SN-GQD/001T.
Hydrogen production activity of SN-GQD/001T: (a) tests of composite with different loading amount of SN-GQD and (b) long-term stability testing.
UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and full-spectrum transient photocurrents of SN-GQD/001T composites: (a) UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) full-spectrum transient photocurrents.

FT-IR spectra and fluorescence probe test of SN-GQD/001T and 001T and possible mechanism behind the reaction: FT-IR spectra of (a) 001T and (b) SN-GQD/001T with adsorption of water and methanol; FT-IR reaction spectra (c) 001T and (d) SN-GQD/001T that under illumination after adsorption of water and methanol; (e) fluorescence intensity of TA probe in the presence of SN-GQD/001T and 001T under illumination; (f) schematic illustration of the reaction mechanism of SN-GQD/001T for hydrogen production.

head.ce:title: S and N codoped graphene quantum dots decorated on (001)TiO2 to boost surface reaction for photocatalytic hydrogen production

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Reservoir Geology and Exploitation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) composites are popular photocatalysts for hydrogen production from water. Here we report a composite consisting of S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (SN-GQDs) with (001)TiO2 for photocatalytic hydrogen production. In addition to traditional advantages like light absorption promotion and charge transfer improvement, the as-prepared SN-GQDs/(001)TiO2 composite could significantly enhance the surface reaction by promotion of the formation of hydroxyl radicals, which results in the increase of the hydrogen production activity by 40 times. This work reveals the importance of surface reaction promotion by GQDs for hydrogen production, which will help researchers understand relevant systems comprehensively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic hydrogen production from water is one of the most promising pathway to convert solar into chemical energy. Since 1972, TiO2 has been extensively researched for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution (Fujishima and Honda, 1972), owing to its low cost and high stability (Gao et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2018). However, TiO2 still suffers from some drawbacks such as the low efficiency of solar utilization due to its wide bandgap and the serious recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, ultimately leading to poor photocatalytic performance (Mohammed, 2020). Consequently, various modification strategies have been introduced to enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of TiO2, including doping, surface modification, and the construction of hetero- or homojunction (Jing et al., 2022; Mohammed, 2019, 2021, 2022; Mohammed and Ying, 2019; Wageh et al., 2022). Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), consisting of small fragments of graphene, have received considerable attention over the past few years (Jun et al., 2021). GQDs not only inherit the advantages of graphene, such as efficient charge carrier mobility and electrical conductivity, but possess the benefits of quantum dots, including rich functional groups and abundant reactive sites (Kittiratanawasin and Hannongbua, 2016). GQDs can serve as both electron donors and electron acceptors to efficiently regulating the photogenerated charge transfer (Chunyu et al., 2022). Therefore, composites of GQDs/TiO2 can enhance hydrogen evolution rate by improving the photon absorption and facilitating the separation of charge carriers (Yan et al., 2019). However, few researches are focused on the influence of GQDs for the reaction on the TiO2 surface (Mahato et al., 2021; Sudhagar et al., 2016). Notably, a related research on the impact of GQDs on the surface reactions of TiO2 with different exposed crystal surfaces has demonstrated that GQDs enhance hydrogen evolution from (001)TiO2 (001T), while having the opposite effect on (101)TiO2 (101T), which is attributed to the depletion of surface hydroxyl groups (Zhong et al., 2022).

Herein, we report a delicate-designed composite consisting of S and N co-doped GQDs (SN-GQD) and (001)TiO2 for photocatalytic hydrogen production. Not only does SN-GQD boost the photon absorption of TiO2 and promote the separation of photogenerated charge carrier, but also can it speed up the surface reaction of (001)TiO2, ultimately increasing the efficiency of hydrogen generation by almost 40 times.

SN-GQD and 001T are prepared based on previous reports (Zhong et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2011). SN-GQD/001T is synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Various amounts of SN-GQD in a hybrid solution of 20 mL of deionized water and 6 mL of ethanol are mixed with 0.4 g of 001T powder; the prepared solution is then transferred to an autoclave and allowed to react at 140 °C for 4 h to yield the composite. Photocatalytic hydrogen production from water is conducted in a 15 mL tube under a 500 W high-pressure mercury lamp irradiation and with methanol as a sacrificial agent. Further experimental details can be found in the Supplementary Data.

The XRD patterns of the catalysts (Fig. 1 a) demonstrate that both 001T and SN-GQD/001T maintain the typical anatase crystal structure of TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21–1272). The peaks of SN-GQD are not observed in the XRD, which can be attributed to the low loading amount and its high dispersion. The Raman spectrum of 001T displays four typical peaks at 147, 400, 519, and 640 cm−1, further confirming the anatase structure of 001T (Fig. 1b) (Sudhagar et al., 2016; Rajender et al., 2018). Notably, the Raman spectrum of SN-GQD/001T exhibits two additional peaks at 1376 and 1590 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1b), originating from the carbon structure of SN-GQD (Sudhagar et al., 2016; Rajender et al., 2018; Park et al., 2009). This indicates the successful preparation of the SN-GQD/001T composite.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of SN-GQD/001T show a lamellar structure, consistent with the typical morphology of TiO2 dominated by the (001) facet (Zhong et al., 2022). The size of SN-GQD on the surface of 001T is closed to 2–3 nm (yellow circle) (Fig. 1c). Additionally, the lattice spacing of SN-GQD is 0.22 nm (Fig. 1d), which closely resembles that of the graphene (100) diffraction facet. This suggests that the SN-GQD loaded on the surface of 001T possesses a highly graphitized carbon structure (Rahbar et al., 2019).

In order to obtain more surface information on SN-GQD/001T, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is conducted. A comprehensive survey of XPS results clearly indicates the presence of Ti, O, C, N and S (Fig. 2 a). Specifically, the C1s spectrum of the composite is fitted to three peaks (Fig. 2b). One of the peaks at 284.8 eV corresponds to the sp2 hybridized carbon in the backbone of the GQDs, while the peaks at 286.3 and 288.6 eV are attributed to hydroxyl carbon and carboxyl carbon functional groups. In Fig. 2c, the peaks at 529.7 and 530.2 eV are identified to Ti–O and O–H bonds, respectively. The loading of SN-GQD on 001T is further confirmed with fine spectra of N1s and S2p showing graphitic N atoms (Zhao et al., 2021) and sulfur in SO bond (Kundu et al., 2015) in the composite (Fig. 2d and e). Moreover, the adsorption of H2O on the surface of the SN-GQD/001T is investigated by temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of H2O (Fig. 2f), which reveals that most of the H2O on the surface existed as intact water molecules, with a small amount of hydroxyl groups, and the introduction of SN-GQD does not significantly promote water adsorption (Fig. S1).

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of SN-GQD/001T with varying weight ratios of SN-GQD (ranging from 0.02 to 1) and 001T is evaluated (Fig. 3 a). The photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of 001T alone is 0.48 mmol g−1 h−1, while SN-GQD is inactive for hydrogen production. Upon the introduction of SN-GQD onto 001T, a significant increase in the hydrogen production rate is observed, reaching a maximum rate of 19.2 mmol g−1 h−1 at a weight ratio of 0.1 SN-GQD to 001T. This represents a 40-fold enhancement compared to bare 001T. Notably, this result also surpasses the hydrogen evolution performance of several other similar carbon/titanium dioxide composites (Table S1). The activity gradually decreases with further increase in SN-GQD content. Excessive loading of SN-GQD results in the reduced incident light absorption due to its opacity and light scattering properties (Xie et al., 2017). Additionally, it masks active sites on the surface, leading to the decreased activity (Ren et al., 2022). Furthermore, long-term hydrogen production tests demonstrate that SN-GQD/001T maintain a good stability beyond 30 h (Fig. 3b), further indicating the excellence of the SN-GQD/001T composites developed in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for the promotion of H2 production
ce:section.ce:para: The enhancement of hydrogen production activity associated with loading SN-GQD may be attributed to improved light absorption, the promotion of charge separation, and the facilitation of surface reactions. In our case, the result from UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 4 a) indicates that the enhancement of the light absorption by 001T with the addition of the SN-GQD is minimal possibly due to the small amount of SN-GQD. Additionally, the transient photocurrent of SN-GQD/001T only increases by 3 times (Fig. 4b), which is significantly lower than the 40-fold enhancement observed for hydrogen evolution. These findings suggest that the improvement in light absorption and charge transfer may not be the sole factors contributing to the increased activity of SN-GQD/001T.

Accordingly, the research focuses on the surface adsorption and reaction of SN-GQD/001T. The kinetic characteristics of the in situ Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples are initially examined in the presence of methanol and water in the dark, which closely resembles the photocatalytic reaction condition. In Fig. 5 a and b, the peaks within the 3100-3600 cm−1 range can be attributed to the H2O with rich hydrogen bonds and Ti–OH (Sudhagar et al., 2016), while that of at 2800∼3000 cm−1 can be attributed to the adsorption of methanol by SN-GQD/001T and 001T (Lin et al., 2019). A comparison of the spectra of SN-GQD/001T and 001T indicates that SN-GQD doesn't significantly enhance the adsorption of methanol and water remarkably, which is consistent with the H2O-TPD results. However, a notable difference in the FTIR spectra of SN-GQD/001T and 001T occurs under light illumination. In comparison to 001T, SN-GQD/001T exhibits faster depletion of Ti–OH and hydrogen bondings as well as sharper methanol desorption (Fig. 5c and d), suggesting rapid surface reactions of water and methanol.

It is reported that water and methanol can readily react with photogenerated holes to generate hydroxyl radicals on the surface of the material (H2O + h+ → ∙OH + H+; CH3OH + h+ → ∙OH + CH3 +) (Ma et al., 2022). To verify this in our case, the fluorescence probe terephthalic acid (TA) was used to analyze the amount of hydroxyl radicals ∙OH generated in the system (Fig. 5e). The emission intensity of probe molecules significantly increases for SN-GQD/001T, indicating a greater amount of ∙OH generated by the photocatalyst. Therefore, SN-GQD may not promote the adsorption of the reaction substrate, but it does facilitate the splitting process of water or methanol to yield ∙OH.

Accordingly, we conjecture that the photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen evolution is promoted in two aspects by SN-GQD. On the one hand, the dissociation of water and methanol produces hydroxyl radicals by consuming a significant number of photogenerated holes. This process effectively decreases the electron-hole recombination and allows for more electrons to contribute to the production of H2; on the other hand, the production of hydroxyl radicals is concomitant with the production of H+, leading to an increase in proton concentration. As a result of the high electron concentration and proton concentration, the hydrogen evolution rate is significantly enhanced. Furthermore, recent reports indicate a significant correlation between surface hydroxyl groups and the hydrogen bonding network (Ma et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2016). Hydrogen bonding networks not only lower the reaction energy barriers, but also promote the proton coupled electron transfer (PCET) process, which may also facilitate the hydrogen production process (Fig. 5f).

In summary, we report a composite of SN-GQD and 001T with remarkably enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. The enhanced activity is mainly attributed to the promotion of the generation of the surface species ∙OH, in addition to increased light absorption and charge separation. This work underscores the significance of promoting surface reactions in the SN-GQD/001T composite for hydrogen production, providing a valuable reference for future studies.

Mengyao Fu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Shan Yu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yunqian Zhong: Data curation, Conceptualization. Yijiang Chen: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Chao Duan: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Heng Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ying Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22002123, 22211530070, 22178291 and 22109132).
